headlineSummary: Hacker News sentiment skews negative for Microsoft Copilot on direct policy complaints, while Anthropic and OpenAI land in a close, mixed-neutral cluster with both praise and criticism [E5] [E6] [E1] [E2] [E3] [E4].

ranking.summary: Microsoft Copilot drew clearly negative comments, with users rejecting OS-level integration and saying it should be fully disableable [E5] [E6].

Anthropic is mixed but slightly more positive than negative in tone, with strong praise for Claude Code alongside sharp criticism over training-data and legal issues, and its position is in a close tie with OpenAI [E1] [E2] [E3].

OpenAI is mostly neutral with limited evidence, combining one positive report about ChatGPT helping produce working code with one comparison that implies it lags behind Claude, and it is in a close tie with Anthropic [E1] [E4].

evidence.excerpt: I am not sure how I feel about all these hype-driven tools honestly, especially considering they are super janky since probably rushed out with Claude Code. It reminds me that I don't really like Anthropic as a company, I just like Claude as a model a lot. It just feels more capable and personable than the others. I wonder if / when OpenAI et al. will be able to replicate it.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly says they do not like Anthropic as a company, but also says they like Claude a lot and find it more capable and personable than alternatives, making the overall stance mixed.
By saying Claude feels more capable/personable and wondering when OpenAI will be able to replicate it, the commenter implies OpenAI is currently behind.

evidence.excerpt: This is the reason why AI companies won't let anyone inspect which content was in the training set. It turns out the suspicions from many copyright holders (including the FSF) was true (of course). Anthropic and others will never admit it, hence why they wanted to settle and not risk going to trial. AI boosters obviously will continue to gaslight copyright holders to believe nonsense like: "It only scraped the links, so AI didn't directly train on your content!", or "AI can't see like humans, it only see numbers, binary or digits" or "AI didn't reproduce exactly 100% of the content just like humans do when tracing from memory!". They will not share the data-set used to train Claude, even if it was trained on AGPLv3 code.

annotations.rationale: The commenter accuses Anthropic of settling to avoid trial, gaslighting copyright holders, and hiding Claude's training data, which is strong criticism on IP/legal grounds.

evidence.excerpt: Claude Code and its ilk can turn "maybe one day" internal projects into live features after a single hour of work. You really, honestly, and truly are missing out if you're not looking for valuable things like that!

annotations.rationale: This explicitly praises Claude Code as enabling major productivity gains very quickly and says others are missing out if they ignore it.

evidence.excerpt: ChatGPT spat out a working Perl prototype based on a C file for Arduino. It quite slow with not very much to optimize, so I made it write it with XS. A hour later I have a working XS implementation that compiles and tests cleanly.

annotations.rationale: The commenter reports successful, useful output from ChatGPT, including a working prototype and then a working XS implementation produced quickly.

evidence.excerpt: No one wants copilot. You can make it an app, but any OS level integration is a non-starter.

annotations.rationale: In the Windows story context, 'copilot' clearly refers to Microsoft's Copilot, and the commenter flatly rejects it, especially OS-level integration.

evidence.excerpt: So still nothing about local accounts, or disabling copilot entirely. This is basically: we’re doing the absolute minimum possible to claim we’re listening to users while still pursuing exactly what we were doing before. We realized we just need to boil the frog a little slower.

annotations.rationale: The commenter complains that Copilot still cannot be disabled entirely and frames Microsoft's approach as minimally responsive and manipulative.
